title: ‘Can’t waste a crisis’: NAB’s reform call
full_text: Other Editions 05 May 2026 MarketWatch ‘Can’t waste a crisis’: NAB’s reform call The Australian David Ross 5 May 2026 727 words English AUSTLN 19 © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. National Australia Bank chief executive Andrew Irvine said his business customers should be free to raise their prices, because the alternative is even worse for the economy. “Business has to make a profit. They can’t trade insolvent,” Mr Irvine said on Monday, after NAB ruled off a $2.63bn interim cash profit and loaded up on provisions against bad debts. The bank boss is uniquely sensitive to the delicate balance suppliers and growers must strike as their costs blow out, but nor can they simply jack up shelf prices which are ultimately influenced by supermarkets. And as the growing crisis in the Strait of Hormuz weaves its way through the supply chain, the result is sharper inflation dragging interest rates higher and dialling up the pressure on loan repayments. NAB had already warned investors in April that its interim result would incur a $706m impairment charge, flowing from the effects of the Iran war on borrowers. It added a further $148m to brace for a “downside economic scenario”, which the bank figured is a 45 per cent probability. “We certainly have an inflation problem and we’re compounding that with a requirement to actually bring demand down a little bit to match supply,” Mr Irvine said. NAB is expecting inflation to rise and economic growth to slow to just 1.5 per cent in 2026. Mr Irvine said the Albanese government should use the looming economic crisis as an opportunity for reform. “We can’t waste a crisis, this is a massive opportunity for us, I hope that’s what we do,” he said. The Canadian banker, who took the reins at NAB in April 2024, said Australia lacked the supply to grow at levels achieved in the past. This comes amid criticism of the NDIS for driving spending and stoking demand, which necessitated a $50bn overhaul of the scheme by federal Labor. The Reserve Bank of Australia will meet today amid broad expectations it will vote to increase rates a third time this year from the present 4.1 per cent. Inflation clipped 4.6 per cent in March. “We’ve got to get inflation under control,” Mr Irvine urged. Transport industry borrowers are also a source of concern for NAB, reflected in the higher forward-looking adjustment. Transport customers join agriculture, manufacturing, construction and commercial property borrowers screening poorly for asset quality. Mr Irvine said many of NAB’s home loan customers were ahead on their mortgages, and sustaining their repayments at levels set before rates tumbled. He agreed there are risks for private credit lenders in Australia if the economic cycle continued to deteriorate, in what could mirror the distress witnessed in parts of the US lending market. He said private credit could face a solvency and liquidity hit from any pullback. “In the US we’ve seen where investors get twitchy and draw on their funds and that means funds stop redemptions. I think that whole scenario is something we’ll see play out if we get a lower growth environment in Australia.” Withstanding pressure from its competitors, NAB still grew its loan book with business lending strongest. Total loans were up 2.9 per cent over the six months to March 31 and Australia’s largest business lender reported a 5.6 lift in commercial lending over the half. Despite the turmoil, NAB expects business credit to grow by almost 8 per cent this year. Home lending also advanced, with NAB reporting it was writing more loans in-house, with nearly 47.7 per cent of new mortgages done via its proprietary channels instead of using brokers. Three years ago, NAB was writing just 35.3 per cent of home loans in-house. Its net interest margin was up 3 basis points in the period, to 1.81 per cent. NAB noted this reflected higher earnings from deposits and lower deposit costs, offset by lending competition. However, expenses were higher up 26.2 per cent. Excluding a significant accounting adjustment, NAB said its expense line was actually lower by half a per cent thanks to productivity benefits. NAB will pay an 85c interim dividend, fully franked, unchanged from last year. The stock closed at $39.20, down 63c. Westpac reports its interim profit today. summary: Older Australians will face an increase in private health insurance costs exceeding $1600 from April 2027 due to the Albanese government's decision to eliminate higher rebates for those over 65. NAB's CEO Andrew Irvine has called for businesses to raise prices to maintain profitability amid rising inflation and economic pressures. He emphasised the need for government reform to address the economic challenges posed by current inflation rates and supply chain issues.
publication:
Seniors to cop a $1600 health cover hike
Daily Telegraph05 May 2026279 wordsJulie Cross

Older Australians are facing private health insurance price hikes of more than $1600 from April next year, under the Albanese government’s plan to scrap higher rebates for over-65s.
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Private health insurance costs for over-65s will increase by over $1600 from April 2027.
NAB's CEO advocates for price increases by businesses to ensure profitability amid inflation.
The Albanese government is urged to use the economic crisis as an opportunity for reform.
relevance: Understanding the impact of policy changes on health insurance costs and economic conditions is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals in assessing risk and advising clients.
